danger_signals.visual_form: Bizarre inanimate objects animated with hostile intent (robotic rabbits, happy faces, cigarettes firing matches, petrol containers, matchboxes)
danger_signals.meaning: Cosmic radiation has corrupted ordinary objects into threats; world is under siege by corrupted matter

danger_signals.visual_form: Visible incoming fire streams from enemies (matches from matchboxes, missiles from petrol containers, standard projectiles)
danger_signals.meaning: Immediate threat requiring evasion or destruction of source

danger_signals.visual_form: Grotesque or unusual large forms (final boss is more grotesque in US version; mid-boss appears as value pack/robotic object; baby-like form in JP version)
danger_signals.meaning: Peak danger nodes; world state corruption manifested as large aberrations

danger_signals.visual_form: Fire pillars from lighters (JP) or refinery stations (US); environmental obstacles
danger_signals.meaning: Environmental corruption of infrastructure; passive threats embedded in world

safety_signals.visual_form: Floating circles with numbers (1-5 for weapons) and letters (F, T, B, W for bombs); 'Power P' items for upgrades; money floating or dropped by defeated enemies
safety_signals.meaning: Progression and empowerment; player gaining capacity to survive and restore order

safety_signals.visual_form: Wing upgrade item that enlarges the player's ship
safety_signals.meaning: Increased survivability; world state stabilization through player capability expansion

safety_signals.visual_form: Transition screen with money-based purchasing system
safety_signals.meaning: Safe harbor; civilization still functional enough to conduct commerce

safety_signals.visual_form: Stage progression through elemental planets (Moon, Fire, Water, Wood, Metal, Earth, Sun)
safety_signals.meaning: Incremental restoration of order; systematic purging of corruption across world

faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Planetary system IOTA Synthetica (artificial, up-scale, hip); functional civilization between levels with shop system

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Corrupted inanimate objects (rabbits, happy faces, cigarettes, matchboxes, petrol containers, lighters/refineries). Regional variation: JP uses domestic/smoking objects; US uses fuel/industrial objects
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Cosmic radiation wave that animates ordinary matter; spreads across all eight planetary stages

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Seven planets and artificial sun in tranquil galactic system; factories producing goods
Strange creatures mixing with factory output; inanimate objects becoming hostile; increasing grotesqueness of enemy forms across stages
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemies attack progressively; corruption manifests as increasingly complex boss forms; factories taken over

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Each of eight stages represents a different planetary domain (Moon, Fire, Water, Wood, Metal, Earth, Sun) with corrupted enemies native to that environment
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Regional enemy types (fire-stage uses fire-themed corrupted objects; later stages show increased complexity)

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Planet of Sun interior; boss forms progressively more grotesque/aberrant (JP version: baby-like form; US version: more grotesque mechanical form)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Multi-phase boss encounters; maximum threat intensity

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Mage returns to planet; summons withdraw; everyday life resumes
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Cosmic power dissipates; world returns to normal; civilization resumes regular function

environmental_storytelling.example: Eight stages named after classical elements in weekday order (Moon→Fire→Water→Wood→Metal→Earth→Sun interior), creating cyclical structure that maps cosmic infection across an entire planetary system

Regional differences encode world-state interpretation: JP version uses everyday smoking/household objects (cigarettes, matchboxes, lighters) to show how mundane becomes monstrous; US version uses industrial/fuel infrastructure (petrol containers, refinery stations) to show how civilization's own systems betray it

Final boss first phase differs by region (JP: baby-like suggesting perversion of life; US: grotesque mechanical form suggesting technology corrupted) — both communicate that corruption inverts the natural/artificial divide
Shop system between stages indicates infrastructure survival; money economy still functional despite crisis; suggests world-state is critical but not apocalyptic
Opening exposition explains peace→threat→investigation failure→factory takeover→civilian death→defense unit defeat→magical summoning; visual progression mirrors this narrative arc

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Single-hit destruction threshold; standard weapon only; slow upgrade rate
Two-hit survivability; access to higher weapon upgrade tiers; increased viability against corruption
Fire projectiles in patterns; move in formation; drop money on destruction; no intelligent evasion
Sustained attack patterns; higher durability than common enemies; thematic tie to stage environment (fire-stage matchbox)
Grotesque form; multi-phase encounter structure; highest attack complexity; source of all corruption (suggested by location at sun - center of system)
Escalating attack patterns; suggests entity has adaptive/learning nature; requires sustained weaponry advancement to defeat
Passive; remain until collected; no threat; indicate safe progression paths

notes: Gun-Nac uses a surreal visual language unique to shmup genre: anthropomorphized inanimate corruption rather than biological aliens. This creates dark comedy (fighting angry rabbits, smiling faces) while communicating genuine existential threat (ordinary matter made hostile by cosmic radiation). Regional differences are significant — JP version emphasizes domestic/everyday corruption (smoking culture refs: cigarettes, matchboxes, lighters); US version emphasizes industrial/technological corruption (fuel, refineries). This suggests localized interpretation of same underlying threat: what you know can kill you. The elemental stage progression (modeled on classical 5-element system + 2 additional) creates a cosmological restoration arc — not random stages but systematic purging of corruption across defined cosmic domains. Only Japanese version provides full narrative context; US version strips opening/ending/plot text, leaving only pure shmup mechanics and visual language. This creates tonal difference: JP = magical girl story about summoned warrior saving worlds; US = abstract space shooter. The 'mage' framing (JP narrative only) explains player's alien power source and temporary nature of victory. Post-ending statement ('power vested within her vanished alongside the fighter jet') suggests world-state returns to baseline vulnerability — corruption threat is cyclical, not permanently resolved.
